1: The opening phrase wayhî ʾaḥᵃrê môt šāʾûl — 'Now it came to pass after the death of Saul' — uses the narrative waw-consecutive perfect (wayhî) to anchor the entire book of 2 Samuel within the 1 Samuel narrative. The clause is the same formula used in Joshua 1:1 after Moses's death, signaling a moment of succession and new divine initiative. The narrator's temporal anchoring ('two days in Ziklag') creates exact chronological continuity with 1 Samuel 30:1.
2: The mourning signs — bᵉgādāyw qᵉrûʿîm wᵉʾădāmāh ʿal-rōʾšô, clothes torn and earth (ʾădāmāh) on his head — are the standard ANE lamentation gestures (see also Job 2:12; Joshua 7:6). The word ʾădāmāh here is precisely soil/earth, not the common ʿāpār (dust); the man has placed actual ground-dirt on his head as a symbol of desolation and mortality.
3: David's interrogation formula — mēʾayin tāḇôʾ, 'where have you come from?' — is a standard narrative inquiry used throughout the Samuel narrative (cf. 1 Samuel 25:11; 2 Samuel 30:13). The Amalekite's self-identification as a survivor from 'the camp of Israel' (maḥănēh yiśrāʾēl) is ambiguous: he positions himself as affiliated with Israel, setting up the theological problem of his subsequent admission.
4: The report uses the verb nûs (fled) for the troops and nāpal (fell/died) for the casualties — the same vocabulary as 1 Samuel 31:1. The Amalekite's account essentially summarizes 1 Samuel 31:1-6, though with crucial divergences in the killing of Saul. The word nāpal carries both the sense of 'fell in battle' and the theological resonance of a fall from divine favor.
5: David's follow-up question — ʾêḵ yādaʿtā, 'how do you know' — presses for verification before acting. This caution mirrors the judicial principle in Deuteronomy 17:6 (requiring two or three witnesses). The narrative is signaling that David is establishing the evidential basis before drawing conclusions about the LORD's anointed.
6: The Amalekite's account diverges from the 1 Sam 31 narrator's version: there, Saul falls on his own sword (1 Samuel 31:4); here the Amalekite claims to have killed him at Saul's request. The phrase nišʿan ʿal-ḥᵃnîtô — 'leaning on his spear' — appears in the narrator's description in 1 Samuel 31:3-4, lending some authenticity to the report. Whether the man is lying for reward or telling the truth is a classic interpretive crux, but the narrative evaluates his claim by his own words (2 Samuel 1:16).
7: The particle hinnenî — 'here I am' — is the standard response to a superior's call, used throughout the OT for moments of readiness before a superior or God (Genesis 22:1; 1 Samuel 3:4-6). The Amalekite's use of it to Saul signals his readiness to serve the king — and thus his active participation in the subsequent events, whatever actually happened.
8: Saul's question mî ʾāttāh — 'who are you?' — and the Amalekite's answer ʿAmlēqî ʾānōḵî — 'I am an Amalekite' — is the narrative's sharpest irony. Saul's downfall began with his failure to fully destroy the Amalekites (1 Sam 15); now an Amalekite stands at his death. The ethnic marker here carries the full theological weight of 1 Samuel 15:3 and 2 Samuel 28:18.
9: The Amalekite quotes Saul's death-request: ʿomdāh-nāʾ ʿālây ûmōtᵉtênî — 'stand over me and kill me.' The reason given — kî ʾᵃḥāzanî haššᵉḇāṣ — contains the noun šᵉḇāṣ, a near-hapax (appearing only here and possibly Psalms 73:6) of disputed meaning: 'anguish,' 'dizziness,' 'convulsion,' or 'cramp.' The MKT renders 'anguish' — this captures the semantic range better than 'agony' (which implies physical pain primarily) or 'dizziness' (too mild). The phrase kî-ḵol-ʿôd napšî bî, 'for my life still clings to me,' uses nepeš in its primary sense of animating life-force, not soul: Saul's life has not yet departed despite his severe wounds.
10: The Amalekite's account of taking the royal insignia: nezer (crown/diadem) and ʾeṣʿādāh (armband). The nezer is the same term used for the high priestly diadem (Exodus 29:6; Leviticus 8:9) and for the Nazirite's consecration crown (Numbers 6:7) — it marks a person set apart and consecrated to YHWH. Bringing it to David is implicitly a transfer of royal legitimacy, but also a dangerous claim to have handled the person of the LORD's anointed. The narrator does not evaluate the claim's truth; the theological verdict comes in David's response (2 Samuel 1:14).
11: David's immediate response — wayyaḥᵃzēq dāwiḏ biḇgādāyw wayyiqrāʿēm, 'David took hold of his clothes and tore them' — is genuine mourning, not performance. The verb ḥāzaq (to grasp firmly) before the tearing intensifies the gesture. The mourning extends to 'all the men with him' (kol-hāʾᵃnāšîm ʾᵃšer ʾittô) — solidarity in grief is a narrative marker of authentic communal response.
12: The mourning sequence — wayyispeḏû wayyiḇkû wayyāṣûmû — uses three waw-consecutive imperfects: they lamented, wept, and fasted. The verb sāpaḏ is the technical term for formal mourning-rites, including breast-beating and rhythmic lamentation; it is the root from which Zechariah's 'mourning for him whom they have pierced' (wᵉsāpeḏû ʿālāyw, Zechariah 12:10) is drawn. The fasting 'until evening' (ʿad-hāʿereḇ) is the standard diurnal marker for mourning fasts (Judges 20:26; 1 Samuel 31:13).
13: David's follow-up identification of the Amalekite — ben-ʾîš gēr ʿᵃmālēqî, 'son of a resident alien, an Amalekite' — uses the legal category gēr (resident foreigner). The gēr lived under Israelite law and enjoyed its protections, but was not a full Israelite. His identity as both gēr (thus subject to Israelite norms) and Amalekite (thus carrying theological freight) is the double basis for David's verdict in 2 Samuel 1:16.
14: David's charge is framed around the theological category of the anointed: lō yārēʾtā liqlōaḥ yādᵉḵā lᵉšaḥēt ʾet-mᵉšîaḥ YHWH — 'you were not afraid to lift your hand to destroy the anointed of the LORD.' The term mᵉšîaḥ (anointed one) denotes someone whose person has been consecrated by the rite of anointing with oil — a rite that transfers them from the common to the sacred sphere. Throughout the David narrative, this category functions as an absolute protection: 1 Samuel 24:6, 10; 2 Samuel 26:9, 11, 23. David consistently refuses to raise his hand against Saul on this ground. The sacral inviolability of the anointed is not political calculation but theological principle: YHWH's act of anointing creates an obligation that supersedes all other considerations.
15: The execution order — lēḵ pᵉgaʿ-bô, 'go, strike him down' — uses pāgaʿ (to fall upon, strike), the same verb used in 1 Samuel 22:17-18 for Doeg's killing of the priests. The immediate compliance and the simple report of death (wayyakkēhû wayyāmōt) is narrative shorthand: judicial death without extended account.
16: The verdict formula: dāmᵉḵā ʿal-rōʾšᵉḵā — 'your blood is on your own head.' This idiom (dām + ʿal + rōʾš) shifts bloodguilt formally from the executioner to the executed: it is the language of Levitical law regarding self-incrimination and bloodguilt transfer. The Amalekite has condemned himself by his own mouth (kî pîḵā ʿānāh bᵉḵā, 'your own mouth testified against you') — his admission of killing the LORD's anointed is the basis of the verdict, not David's independent knowledge of what actually happened.
17: The narrative transition to the lament uses wayyəqōnēn — the piel of qûn, to chant a dirge. The qînāh (dirge-elegy) is a specific literary form distinguished by its 3+2 metrical pattern (longer half-line + shorter half-line), named after this word. The form itself enacts mourning through rhythmic imbalance — the shortened second colon mimics incompleteness, the truncation of life.
18: David commands the lament to be taught to 'the sons of Judah' (bᵉnê yᵉhûḏāh) and notes it is 'written in the Book of Jashar' (sēper hayyāšār). The verb lᵉlammed (to teach, piel infinitive construct) indicates this is deliberate cultural formation: official mourning poetry to be memorized and performed. The sēper hayyāšār is also cited in Joshua 10:13; it appears to have been an anthology of heroic poetry about Israel's leaders, now lost. The title of the poem — haqqeŝet, 'the bow' — is named for Jonathan's weapon (2 Samuel 1:22), not merely for the literary instruction 'to teach the bow.'
19: The opening line of the dirge: haṣṣᵉḇî yiśrāʾēl ʿal-bāmôtêḵā ḥālāl — 'your beauty/glory, O Israel, lies slain on your high places.' The noun ṣᵉḇî means both 'gazelle' (the animal) and 'glory/beauty/honor' — the same ambiguity as in 2 Samuel 1:19 and 2 Samuel 23:1 (nᵉʾûm haggeber huqqam ʿāl). The 'high places' (bāmôt) is the topographic term for elevated worship/battle sites; Gilboa is specified later (2 Samuel 1:21). The refrain ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm — 'how the mighty have fallen' — uses the exclamatory ʾêḵ (how!) of lament poetry, also in Lamentations 1:1; 2 Samuel 2:1; 2 Samuel 4:1.
20: The honor-shame geography of the prohibition: ʾal-taggîḏû ḇᵉḡat ʾal-tᵉḇaśśᵉrû bᵉḥûṣôt ʾašqᵉlôn — 'tell it not in Gath, proclaim it not in the streets of Ashkelon.' Gath and Ashkelon are Philistine cities; Gath most proximate to Israelite territory. The double prohibition uses the jussive (ʾal + imperfect) — a softer prohibition implying 'would that it were not' — and the paired verbs nāgad (to tell) and bāśar (to bring news/tidings) are both technically positive report verbs here used negatively. The daughters of the Philistines rejoicing (tiśmaḥnāh) inverts the standard Israelite victory song pattern (1 Samuel 18:6-7).
21: The curse on Gilboa: hārê baggillᵉbōaʿ ʾal-ṭal wᵉʾal-māṭār ʿᵃlêḵem — 'Mountains of Gilboa, let neither dew nor rain fall on you.' This imprecatory formula uses the jussive and echoes the cosmic judgment language of Deuteronomy 11:17 and 1 Kings 8:35, where withheld rain signals divine disfavor. The phrase śᵉḏê tᵉrûmōt (NIV 'fields of grain offerings'; MKT 'fields yield first-fruit offerings') is difficult: tᵉrûmāh normally denotes a contribution/heave-offering, not a field product. The line may mean: let there be no agricultural produce to offer — curse of barrenness on the killing ground. The clause māgēn šāʾûl bᵉlî māšîaḥ baššāmen — 'the shield of Saul, no longer anointed with oil' — treats the battle-shield as a consecrated object that receives ritual oiling (1 Samuel 21:9 notes Goliath's spear; ANE shields were oiled for maintenance and possibly ritual protection). Saul's shield now lies unoiled — abandoned and desacralized.
22: The paired weapons line: middam ḥᵃlālîm meḥēleḇ gibbôrîm qeŝet yᵉhônātān lōʾ nāšôg ʾāḥôr — 'from the blood of the slain, from the fat of the warriors, the bow of Jonathan turned not back.' The verse celebrates military effectiveness through the image of weapons sated with enemy blood and fat. The meḥēleḇ ('from the fat') employs the same term as animal sacrifice (ḥēleḇ, the fat portions offered to YHWH) — a subtle elevation of battle prowess to the language of sacred offering. The bow of Jonathan 'did not turn back' (lōʾ nāšôg) — nāšag means to recede or retreat, usually used of armies; the personification of the weapon as a faithful warrior is a characteristic feature of martial poetry.
23: The elegiac portrait of Saul and Jonathan together: hammᵉʾāhāḇîm wᵉhanneʿîmīm bᵉḥayyêhem ûḇᵉmôtām lōʾ niprāḏû — 'beloved and lovely in life, and in death they were not separated.' The participle mᵉʾāhāḇîm (beloved) is the passive pual, implying their belovedness was recognized by others; neʿîmîm (lovely/pleasant) is the adjective from the root used in Psalms 16:6 ('pleasant lines') and Song of Solomon 1:16. The comparison to eagles (minnešārîm qallû, 'swifter than eagles') and lions (mēʾᵃrāyôt gāḇērû, 'stronger than lions') deploys Israel's standard martial excellence idiom.
24: The call to the daughters of Israel (bᵉnôt yiśrāʾēl) to weep for Saul — hammalbiššᵉḵem šānî ʿim-ʿáḏānîm, 'who clothed you in scarlet with ornaments' — shifts from martial to patronage imagery. Saul as provider of luxury clothing for the women of Israel is a direct reversal of the honor-shame logic of 2 Samuel 1:20 (the daughters of the Philistines). The king's largesse (ʿáḏānîm, from the root meaning pleasure/luxury) is framed as a reason for public mourning — the patron-benefactor logic that structures ANE kingship.
25: The repetition of ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm — the refrain from 2 Samuel 1:19 — with the addition of bᵉtôḵ hammilḥāmāh, 'in the thick of battle.' The Hebrew prepositional phrase bᵉtôḵ (in the midst of) intensifies the pathos: Jonathan did not die on the margins or in retreat but at the center of the fight.
26: The most intimate verse of the lament: ṣar-lî ʿālêḵā ʾāḥî yᵉhônātān nāʿamtā-llî mᵉʾōḏ niplᵉʾātāh ʾahᵃḇātᵉḵā lî middᵉʾahᵃḇat nāšîm — 'I grieve for you, my brother Jonathan; you were most dear to me; your love for me was more extraordinary than the love of women.' The word niplᵉʾātāh is the nifal perfect of pālaʾ — 'to be extraordinary, surpassing, wonderful' — the same root used in Psalms 139:6 ('too wonderful for me') and Exodus 34:10 ('wonderful things'). The phrase does not make a comparative judgment about love itself but about its quality: covenant-loyalty love (ʾahᵃḇāh in the covenantal sense established in 1 Samuel 18:1-4) that is extraordinary in its constancy — Jonathan's giving up his royal rights (1 Samuel 18:4) and repeated protection of David at personal risk constitutes this niplāʾ quality.
27: The closing refrain: ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm wayyōʾḇᵉḏû kᵉlê milḥāmāh — 'how the mighty have fallen, and the weapons of war perished!' The verb ʾāḇad (perished) echoes the noun form in many Deuteronomic judgment texts (wᵉʾāḇadtā, 'you will perish'); here the weapons of war are treated as agents whose perishing (kᵉlê milḥāmāh) is the natural end of their wielders' fall. The inclusio formed by 2 Samuel 1:19 and 27 (both using ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm) marks the formal boundaries of the Qinah poem.
